Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First the goal is to get all events from cases that have total events 6 first we need to calculate the count of events per case_id right

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: First option use a subquery with GROUP BY to get the valid case IDs then join back to the original event_log Wait or use a window function

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Wait first lets outline the logic 1 For each case_id count how many events it has 2 Keep only case_ids where total_events_per_case 6 3 Return all rows from event_log where case_id is in that set

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Lets think of two common approaches one with a WHERE IN subquery another with a window function and a WHERE clause on the window count

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Suppose case A has 3 events case B has 7 The first approach would get case As ID then select all rows where case_id is A which is correct The window function approach would add a column thats 3 for all rows of A 7 for B then filter where that column is 6 which also gives all A rows correct

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The problem says each row is a single event so COUNT star is okay right If we wanted to count distinct events but the problem says each row is an event COUNT star is correct

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The problem says returns only the events from cases that have six or fewer events in total yes all columns of event_log for those rows

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: The window function approach SELECT star FROM SELECT star COUNT star OVER PARTITION BY case_id AS total_events_per_case FROM event_log AS counted_events WHERE total_events_per_case 6

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Alternatively the subquery with GROUP BY and join SELECT el star FROM event_log el JOIN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT star 6 AS valid_cases ON el case_id equals valid_cases case_id

Name: Validation - PE
Text: That also works Which one is better Well in DuckDB both are efficient

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: If we used WHERE COUNT star 6 directly that would throw an error because WHERE is evaluated before GROUP BY you cant use aggregate functions in WHERE you need HAVING

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Wait can you use window functions in the WHERE clause Wait no in SQL the WHERE clause is evaluated before the SELECT list window functions cant be used in WHERE you need a subquery or a QUALIFY clause

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: DuckDB supports QUALIFY which is like a WHERE for window functions evaluated after the window functions are computed

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: In DuckDB you can write SELECT star FROM event_log QUALIFY COUNT star OVER PARTITION BY case_id 6

Name: Validation - PE
Text: That is even shorter Because QUALIFY filters the results after window functions are computed you do not need a subquery

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We need to first identify valid case_ids those with total event count 6 then retrieve all events belonging to those cases

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: The standard window function approach with a subquery would return the extra count column if we use outer SELECT star so to avoid returning the extra count column we can instead use another approach

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: SELECT star FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT star 6

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The WHERE IN approach gets all valid case_ids then the outer query selects all rows where case_id is in that list which is correct

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Trying to put COUNT star in the WHERE clause directly would be invalid because WHERE filters rows before aggregation so you need HAVING for that or window functions with QUALIFY

Name: Validation - PE
Text: All these approaches correctly handle cases with exactly 6 events because the condition is 6 not less than 6

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: If you are using DuckDB and want the most concise readable query use the QUALIFY version If you need standard SQL that works across all relational databases use the WHERE IN with grouped subquery approach or the join approach
